addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.

- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). content.text:
Here's the full picture for your setup (Hyprland + hyprpaper, monitor `DP-2`).

## Changing your wallpaper right now (hyprpaper)

You have no `hyprpaper.conf`, so nothing is being set at startup. Two ways:

**One-off, immediately (no restart):**
```bash
hyprctl hyprpaper preload "$HOME/Pictures/wall.jpg"
hyprctl hyprpaper wallpaper "DP-2,$HOME/Pictures/wall.jpg"
hyprctl hyprpaper unload unused   # free memory from old images
```

**Persistent** — create `~/.config/hypr/hyprpaper.conf`:
```ini
preload = ~/Pictures/wall.jpg
wallpaper = DP-2,~/Pictures/wall.jpg
splash = false
```
It's already launched from your `hyprland.lua:56`, so it'll pick this up on next login.

## Auto-rotate like macOS

hyprpaper **can't** rotate on a timer — it just holds static images. For the "slideshow from a folder" behaviour, the best option is:

### 🏆 wpaperd — recommended
Purpose-built for exactly this. Point it at a folder, set an interval, done. Config `~/.config/wpaperd/config.toml`:
```toml
[DP-2]
path = "~/Pictures/wallpapers/"   # a folder = rotate through it
duration = "30m"
sorting = "random"
mode = "center"
```
Then run `wpaperd -d`. It handles per-monitor, random/ascending order, and smooth transitions natively. Closest thing to macOS dynamic-desktop rotation.

```bash
sudo pacman -S wpaperd   # (or: paru -S wpaperd-bin from AUR)
```

### Alternatives
- **swww** — gorgeous transition animations (fade, wipe, grow), but rotation isn't built in; you drive it with a tiny loop or systemd timer calling `swww img`. Pick this if you care most about the *transition* effect.
- **waypaper** — a GUI front-end if you'd rather click than edit configs; it can use swww/hyprpaper/wpaperd as a backend.
- **mpvpaper** — only if you want *video/live* wallpapers.

**If you switch to wpaperd**, change your `hyprland.lua:56` from launching `hyprpaper` to `wpaperd -d` (don't run both — they'll fight over the surface).

Want me to install wpaperd, write the config for a specific folder, and swap the launch line in your Lua config?
Let me check what's available and where your images are before making changes.

# "default" applies to every monitor unless a monitor is named explicitly.

path = "~/Pictures/wallpapers"   # a folder => rotate through its images
duration = "30m"                 # how long each wallpaper stays up
sorting = "random"               # random | ascending | descending
mode = "center"                  # center | fit | stretch | tile
transition-time = 700            # crossfade duration in ms

# Example: override a single monitor by name (yours is DP-2)

# path = "~/Pictures/wallpapers"
# duration = "1h"

content.text:
Everything's wired up except the install itself, which needs your password. Run these two in this session using the `!` prefix (type the `!` then the command):

**1. Install wpaperd:**
```
! sudo pacman -S --needed wpaperd
```

**2. Switch over live (no logout needed):**
```
! killall hyprpaper 2>/dev/null; wpaperd -d
```

After that it'll rotate automatically, and it'll autostart on future logins from the Lua change.

## What I set up

| Item | Value |
|------|-------|
| **Installed** | `wpaperd` (you run the install command above) |
| **Wallpaper folder** | `~/Pictures/wallpapers/` — seeded with Hyprland's `wall0/1/2.png` |
| **Config** | `~/.config/wpaperd/config.toml` — rotate **random**, **every 30 min**, 700 ms crossfade |
| **Autostart** | `hyprland.lua:56` changed from `hyprpaper` → `wpaperd -d` |

## Tweaking it later
- **Add your own wallpapers:** just drop images into `~/Pictures/wallpapers/` — no restart needed, wpaperd picks them up.
- **Change the interval / order:** edit `~/.config/wpaperd/config.toml` (`duration`, `sorting`), then `killall wpaperd && wpaperd -d`.
- **Force the next wallpaper now:** `wpaperctl next` (and `wpaperctl previous`).

One note: I left `hyprpaper` installed — it's harmless now that nothing launches it, but say the word if you want me to remove it too. Want me to verify wpaperd is running once you've done the install?

`-Rns` also removes any now-orphaned dependencies and its config from the package (your `~/.config` files are untouched — though there's no `hyprpaper.conf` there anyway). Paste the output back if you want me to confirm it's gone cleanly.

content.text: Ah — the `!` prefix runs commands in this non-interactive session, so `sudo` has
[Request interrupted by user]
No response requested.

message.content: how do i put this computer to sleep ?

input.description: Check for existing suspend keybind in hypr config

content.content: /home/tunc/.config/hypr/hyprland.lua:54:   hl.exec_cmd("systemctl --user start hyprpolkitagent")

content.text:
No sleep keybind is configured. The command is:

```
! systemctl suspend
```

This suspends to RAM (macOS-style sleep) and normally works **without** a password thanks to polkit. Run it via the `!` prefix and your machine should sleep immediately; press a key / power button to wake.

Other variants if you want them:
- `systemctl hibernate` — suspend to disk (needs swap ≥ RAM; off unless configured)
- `systemctl hybrid-sleep` — RAM + disk fallback

Want me to add a **keybind** for it in your Hyprland config (e.g. `Super+Escape` → suspend) so you don't need the command each time?
Let me see how keybinds are defined in your Lua config so I match the style.

content.text: I don't see the keybind section yet — let me find where binds are defined.

input.command: grep -niE 'bind|mainMod|\$mod|keyword' ~/.config/hypr/hyprland.lua | head -30; echo "=== total lines ==="; wc -l ~/.config/hypr/hyprland.lua; echo "=== any other hypr conf files sourced ==="; ls ~/.config/hypr/
input.description: Locate keybind section and other sourced files

content.content:
264:---- KEYBINDINGS ----
267:local mainMod = "SUPER" -- Sets "Windows" key as main modifier
269:-- Example binds, see https://wiki.hypr.land/Configuring/Basics/Binds/ for more
270:hl.bind(mainMod .. " + Q", hl.dsp.exec_cmd(terminal))
271:local closeWindowBind = hl.bind(mainMod .. " + C", hl.dsp.window.close())
272:-- closeWindowBind:set_enabled(false)
273:hl.bind(mainMod .. " + M", hl.dsp.exec_cmd("command -v hyprshutdown >/dev/null 2>&1 && hyprshutdown || hyprctl dispatch 'hl.dsp.exit()'"))
274:hl.bind(mainMod .. " + E", hl.dsp.exec_cmd(fileManager))
275:hl.bind(mainMod .. " + V", hl.dsp.window.float({ action = "toggle" }))
276:hl.bind(mainMod .. " + R", hl.dsp.exec_cmd(menu))
277:hl.bind(mainMod .. " + P", hl.dsp.window.pseudo())
278:hl.bind(mainMod .. " + J", hl.dsp.layout("togglesplit"))    -- dwindle only
280:-- Move focus with mainMod + arrow keys
281:hl.bind(mainMod .. " + left",  hl.dsp.focus({ direction = "left" }))
282:hl.bind(mainMod .. " + right", hl.dsp.focus({ direction = "right" }))
283:hl.bind(mainMod .. " + up",    hl.dsp.focus({ direction = "up" }))
284:hl.bind(mainMod .. " + down",  hl.dsp.focus({ direction = "down" }))
286:-- Switch workspaces with mainMod + [0-9]
287:-- Move active window to a workspace with mainMod + SHIFT + [0-9]
290:    hl.bind(mainMod .. " + " .. key,             hl.dsp.focus({ workspace = i}))
291:    hl.bind(mainMod .. " + SHIFT + " .. key,     hl.dsp.window.move({ workspace = i }))
295:hl.bind(mainMod .. " + S",         hl.dsp.workspace.toggle_special("magic"))
296:hl.bind(mainMod .. " + SHIFT + S", hl.dsp.window.move({ workspace = "special:magic" }))
298:-- Scroll through existing workspaces with mainMod + scroll
299:hl.bind(mainMod .. " + mouse_down", hl.dsp.focus({ workspace = "e+1" }))
300:hl.bind(mainMod .. " + mouse_up",   hl.dsp.focus({ workspace = "e-1" }))
302:-- Move/resize windows with mainMod + LMB/RMB and dragging
303:hl.bind(mainMod .. " + mouse:272", hl.dsp.window.drag(),   { mouse = true })
304:hl.bind(mainMod .. " + mouse:273", hl.dsp.window.resize(), { mouse = true })
307:hl.bind("XF86AudioRaiseVolume", hl.dsp.exec_cmd("wpctl set-volume -l 1 @DEFAULT_AUDIO_SINK@ 5%+"), { locked = true, repeating = true })
=== total lines ===
369 /home/tunc/.config/hypr/hyprland.lua
=== any other hypr conf files sourced ===
hyprland.conf
hyprland.lua

content.text: The bind style is clear. I'll add `SUPER + Escape` → suspend right after the app binds.

input.new_string:
hl.bind(mainMod .. " + J", hl.dsp.layout("togglesplit"))    -- dwindle only
hl.bind(mainMod .. " + Escape", hl.dsp.exec_cmd("systemctl suspend"))  -- sleep

content.text: Now reload Hyprland so the new bind takes effect:

input.command: hyprctl reload 2>&1; echo "---"; hyprctl binds -j 2>/dev/null | grep -i -B2 -A2 'escape' | head -20
input.description: Reload Hyprland and verify the suspend bind is registered

content.content:
ok
---
    "submap": "",
    "submap_universal": "false",
    "key": "Escape",
    "keycode": 0,
    "catch_all": false,

content.text:
Done. **`Super + Escape`** now suspends the machine, and it's live immediately (verified — the bind is registered after reload).

- Added at `hyprland.lua:279`, alongside your other app binds.
- Uses `systemctl suspend`, so no password needed.
- Persists across reboots.

Press `Super + Escape` to test — it should sleep instantly. Tap a key or the power button to wake.
